posts.cooked:
• 2013 Coudert Fleurie Clos de la Roilette - France, Burgundy, Beaujolais, Fleurie (6/14/2015)
    Popped and poured, dark ruby, deep for Beaujolais. Like a bowl of assorted red fruits on the nose…aromas of fresh cherry, crushed raspberries and under-ripened strawberry smelling as though they were smashed through crushed stones. A touch of rhubarb and spice adds complexity. Medium to medium-plus body, great concentration and lively acidity. Initially, the palate is firmly centered on boisterous red fruits complimented by a subtle spiciness from oak but as this keeps aerating a continually growing impression of minerals moves to the forefront. Chalky tannins on the persistent close.
    This will age for many years but is nearly impossible to resist right now, super impressive.

I can’t stop opening these.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Yea, this is really good stuff. I went back to the well for more.
It’s one of those wines that you know will age nicely for a long time but is just soooo yummy right now.

posts.name: Warren Taranow
posts.cooked: How does this differ from the Tardive? I’ve had the Tardive and Griffe du Marquis, and none of those from recent vintages seem near ready.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
Warren Taranow:

How does this differ from the Tardive? I’ve had the Tardive and Griffe du Marquis, and none of those from recent vintages seem near ready.

The Tardive and the Griffe are from older vines and intended to need more age than the straight cuvee. Griffe and Tardive are from the same vines, I believe, but the Griffe is aged longer in foudre, or something like that.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Jonathan Loesberg:
Warren Taranow:

How does this differ from the Tardive? I’ve had the Tardive and Griffe du Marquis, and none of those from recent vintages seem near ready.

The Tardive and the Griffe are from older vines and intended to need more age than the straight cuvee. Griffe and Tardive are from the same vines, I believe, but the Griffe is aged longer in foudre, or something like that.

Actually the Tardive is aged in foudre, whereas the Griffe spends some time in Burgundy barrels.

Great note to begin this post! Had a bottle on Saturday that was showing just as you describe it. It’s in a very expressive place.
I had this last September and it was so closed I almost cringed as I was drinking it. Sounds like it’s opened up a bit. Maybe I need to stock up on more…thank you for the note

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Kirk.Grant:

I had this last September and it was so closed I almost cringed as I was drinking it. Sounds like it’s opened up a bit. Maybe I need to stock up on more…thank you for the note

It has! And pretty much only in the last two months…

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:
Jonathan Loesberg:
Warren Taranow:

How does this differ from the Tardive? I’ve had the Tardive and Griffe du Marquis, and none of those from recent vintages seem near ready.

The Tardive and the Griffe are from older vines and intended to need more age than the straight cuvee. Griffe and Tardive are from the same vines, I believe, but the Griffe is aged longer in foudre, or something like that.

Actually the Tardive is aged in foudre, whereas the Griffe spends some time in Burgundy barrels.

Great note to begin this post! Had a bottle on Saturday that was showing just as you describe it. It’s in a very expressive place.

The website doesn’t, at least in French, mention any foudres for the Tardive (although the LDM site does). It does say barriques de Bourgogne for the Griffe, but the winemaker says “foudre” on the soundtrack.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: My information derives from the long-time UK importer, but Bettane and Dessauve also give the same details. If you look at pictures of the Roillette cellar you can’t miss the foudres. Not sure what soundtrack you’re referring to?

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

My information derives from the long-time UK importer, but Bettane and Dessauve also give the same details. If you look at pictures of the Roillette cellar you can’t miss the foudres. Not sure what soundtrack you’re referring to?

Alain Coudert on his website tells the history of the domaine and briefly describes the three cuvees. Here he is on the three cuvees.

http://www.clos-de-la-roilette.com/index.php?page=vins

In some ways, this wine reminds of the 2009 Lapierre on release. While more structured, rustic and without the smooth, silky mouth feel of the Lapierre, this has quite a bit of similarity in appealing fruit, subtle spice, and nice mineral notes.

Thx for the link!

posts.name: Berry Crawford
posts.cooked:
Man, I need to start buying more Coudert.

Anyone else feel they are almost more like northern rhones in flavor and weight than typical Beaujolais? (not that I think it detracts, its just a different style)

posts.name: Mike S h o r
posts.cooked: Thank you for the note. I loved all three cuvees in 2009 and 2010 but absolutely despised the artificial candy component in the 2012s, so they fell off my radar this year. Will need to try the 2013s.

Berry Crawford:

Anyone else feel they are almost more like northern rhones in flavor and weight than typical Beaujolais? (not that I think it detracts, its just a different style)

I’d say more Grenache-heavy Cotes du Rhone but that’s still near my initial impression of Lapierre the first time I had one, which would have been the 2008. Less raspberry and more cherry from Gamay but with an impression of Rhone-esque spice that seems to lacking from many other Bojos.

It’s so very interesting to compare the different styles of Beaujolais from the better producers.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
AlexS:
Berry Crawford:

Anyone else feel they are almost more like northern rhones in flavor and weight than typical Beaujolais? (not that I think it detracts, its just a different style)

I’d say more Grenache-heavy Cotes du Rhone but that’s still near my initial impression of Lapierre the first time I had one, which would have been the 2008. Less raspberry and more cherry from Gamay but with an impression of Rhone-esque spice that seems to lacking from many other Bojos.

It’s so very interesting to compare the different styles of Beaujolais from the better producers.

Beaujolais has become to me what Southern Rhone once was: Crisp, medium weight, food-friendly, inexpensive wine that delivers high quality. I cannot drink most Rhones now that they all seem to be pushing 15%.
